user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. Plaintiff alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of Money / Refund of Advance

queries:
Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

LawDomain: Other

enrichment_log: Section 65 Indian Contract: FOUND in RAG

placeholder_dates.AGREEMENT_DATE: Date of the agreement/contract between parties
placeholder_dates.ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE: Date when the plaintiff made the payment/advance
placeholder_dates.PERFORMANCE_DATE: Date by which the defendant was obligated to perform
placeholder_dates.DEFAULT_DATE: Date when the defendant failed to repay/perform
placeholder_dates.NOTICE_DATE: Date when legal notice was sent
placeholder_dates.NOTICE_REPLY_DATE: Date when reply to legal notice was received
placeholder_dates.CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE: Date when cause of action first arose

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff instituted this money-recovery action in the competent court at {{COURT_LOCATION}} against the Defendant.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came into contact while negotiating a prospective business transaction for the supply of goods/services. The discussions unfolded at the parties’ respective offices and through telephone and email communications, culminating in a formal written agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} which is documented and referenced as Annexure A. The relationship between the parties thus began as a commercial negotiation that progressed into a contractual relationship upon execution of the Agreement (Annexure A).

2. On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the parties executed the Agreement/Contract (Annexure A). The terms as recorded in the Agreement provided that the Defendant would supply the contracted goods/services described in Annexure A and that the Plaintiff would make an advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000 toward performance of the contract. The Agreement set a performance date of {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} for the Defendant to commence and complete the required delivery, and it provided that, if performance was not completed, the Plaintiff would be entitled to the refund of the advance amount. This narrative reflects the understanding and obligations that the parties purported to undertake under the written contract (Annexure A).

3. In accordance with the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by bank transfer to the account designated by the Defendant. The payment was made in full and is evidenced by the bank-related documents in Annexure B. The transfer was intended as an advance toward performance of the contract and is described in Annexure B as part of the transaction between the Plaintiff and the Defendant.

4. The Defendant undertook to commence performance and deliver the contracted goods/services by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff undertook to assist as reasonably required, including providing any necessary confirmations or approvals to facilitate performance. These reciprocal obligations and the timeline for performance are reflected in the terms of the Agreement (Annexure A).

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform by the stipulated due date and failed to refund the Rs. 2,000,000 advance. Despite the Plaintiff’s repeated requests and communications seeking either delivery of the contracted goods/services or repayment of the advance, the Defendant did not deliver the goods/services nor return the advance amount. The non-performance and non-refund by the Defendant constitute the specific default described in the factual timeline of the contract and the related communications.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs within the specified period, and the notice is attached as Annexure C. The Defendant’s reply to the legal notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} and is contained in Annexure D. Following these communications, the Plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, seeking recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs, with supporting documentation referenced in Annexures A, B, C, D, and F as applicable. Annexure E contains any acknowledgment/admission document, if available, and Annexure F includes additional supporting documents.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a specific restitutionary obligation: when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, on {{DATE}} at {{PLACE}}, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} paid the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} Rs. 20,00,000 as an advance under the Agreement dated {{DATE}}, evidenced by Annexure A, with payment proof in Annexure B. The business transaction contemplated by that agreement failed due to the Defendant’s default in performance, resulting in total failure of consideration; the Defendant has retained the Rs. 20,00,000 without lawful justification and is thus obligated to restore the entire amount to the Plaintiff under the said provision. The legal consequence is restitution: the Defendant must refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000 together with interest and costs as may be fixed by the Court. The Plaintiff accordingly pleads for refund of Rs. 20,00,000 and for costs and interest, relying on Annexure A to show the contract, Annexure B to prove payment, and Annexure C (legal notice) and Annexure D (reply) to reflect the pre-suit dispute and Defendant’s default. The Defendant may advance defenses such as lack of a valid contract, or absence of any part performance or adjustment against the claim; in response, it is submitted that Annexure A confirms a binding contract, Annexure B confirms payment of the admitted advance, there is no forfeiture clause or set-off claim material to defeat restitution, and there is no counter-claim, so the defense cannot nullify the statutory restoration duty under Section 65. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court does not accept total failure of consideration as the basis for relief, the Plaintiff nonetheless seeks restoration of the amount under Section 65 as the proper remedy to prevent unjust enrichment and to place the Plaintiff in the position prior to payment, together with interest and costs. Annexure F may be relied upon for any additional supportive documents.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: Origin of the cause of action arises on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Plaintiff's advance of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rs. 2,000,000) paid for the contemplated business transaction under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and to perform the obligations arising therefrom, thereby resulting in a total failure of consideration and giving the Plaintiff a right to seek repayment of the advance amount with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding refund of the advance, and the Defendant, upon receipt of the notice, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount, a position reflected in the Defendant's reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}; by this act the Defendant reinforced the continuing breach and the Plaintiff’s entitlement to relief. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 20,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and this retention persists as of the filing of the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization, with the Plaintiff thus seeking the immediate refund of the advance amount along with interest and costs to be awarded on the terms of the law. Place names and the manner of retention reflect the ongoing failure of performance by the Defendant and the Plaintiff’s continuing entitlement to the return of its funds. {{PLACE_NAME}} and {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} are referenced to establish the contractual nexus and the defendant’s obligations, while the notices and replies dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} illustrate the sequence of demands and non-compliance that sustain the action throughout the period leading up to filing. The Plaintiff relies on the facts set out here to establish a clear origin, a continued accrual, and the ongoing nature of the wrongful retention of the advance, with the remedy sought including refund, interest, and costs.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, contending total failure of consideration due to the Defendant’s default. The Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to repay the advance in accordance with the contract, thereby giving rise to a cause of action in law. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, when the Defendant failed to perform its obligation. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963; the limitation period commences from the accrual date {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} (the date the cause of action first arose) and the suit has been filed within that period.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: On {{FILING_DATE}} before {{COURT_NAME}} the Plaintiff values the suit at Rs. 2,000,000, being the principal amount claimed against the Defendant. This valuation is for purposes of jurisdiction and court fee computation. In accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, the court fee payable on the plaint is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated by applying the Act’s schedule to the suit value.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff seeks three distinct prayers in the interest claim. Pre-suit interest is claimed on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum for the period from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, arising from the Defendant’s failure to repay or perform in accordance with the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and the advance of Rs. 2,000,000 made on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} for the commercial transaction between the parties. The legal basis rests on contractual understanding and commercial practice that money paid or lent as part of a business transaction bears interest when the debtor fails to honor the obligation, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The period of pre-suit interest thus runs from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} up to the filing of suit on {{FILING_DATE}} and is justified by the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, as evidenced by the contemporaneous agreement and the parties’ conduct leading to demand for repayment.

Pendente lite interest is claimed at {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on Rs. 2,000,000 from the date of filing the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, currently anticipated on {{DECRE_DATE}}. This prayer is made under Section 34 CPC, which permits the Court to award interest on money claimed from the date of the suit until decree at a rate deemed just, not exceeding the contract or law. The rate is justified because the suit concerns a commercial transaction in which the Defendant has benefited from the use of the Plaintiff’s funds during the pendency of the litigation, and the Plaintiff has been deprived of the use of that money. The chosen rate reflects prevailing mercantile norms and, where applicable, the contractual understanding between the parties. The pendente lite period thus spans from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECRE_DATE}}, and the legal basis is Section 34 CPC, with the rate selected to reflect commercial reality and the risk of delay.

Future interest is sought on the decretal amount from the date of decree on {{DECRE_DATE}} until realization on {{REALIZATION_DATE}} at {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum. This post-decree interest is warranted to ensure full compensation for the continued withholding of the money after judgment and to secure the Plaintiff’s ability to recover the amount adjudged, consistent with the overarching objective of Section 34 CPC to compensate for the use of money until realisation. The rate chosen aligns with commercial practice and the need to encourage prompt satisfaction of the decree, and it is justified by the Defendant’s obligation to honor the decree in respect of the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 as adjudged by the Court.

In summary, the three prayers provide for (i) pre-suit interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum with the contractual/commercial justification; (ii) pendente lite interest from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECRE_DATE}} at {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC with justification grounded in the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention; and (iii) future post-decree interest from {{DECRE_DATE}} to {{REALIZATION_DATE}} at {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum to secure realization and reflect the Court’s mandate to compensate for the use of money until full realization.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
(b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} up to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) Award pendente lite interest on the principal sum at the rate of {{PENDENTE_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree. 
(d) Order future interest at the rate of {{FUTURE_RATE}}% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} amounting to Rs. {{COSTS_AMOUNT}}/-. 
(f) Pass any other relief as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Phrase 'interest and costs' repeated 4 times — consider varying language

Phrase 'the advance amount' repeated 4 times — consider varying language
Phrase 'of rs. 2,000,000' repeated 7 times — consider varying language

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff instituted this money-recovery action in the competent court at {{COURT_PLACE}} against the Defendant.


JURISDICTION

Territorial jurisdiction: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, {{STATE}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this District Court. The Plaintiff entered into an agreement with the Defendant on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} which was executed at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}}, {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 as advance for the business transaction on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} through a transfer from the Plaintiff’s account held with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}}, which is located within the local limits of this Court’s jurisdiction. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and the Defendant defaulted in performing the obligations on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}; a legal notice was issued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} and the reply to the notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. The facts establishing the Defendant’s breach are sufficient to confer jurisdiction, and the claim seeks recovery of the advance amount along with costs and interest.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks a total amount of Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within this District Court’s monetary limits for suits of a monetary nature.

Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came into contact while negotiating a prospective business transaction for the supply of goods/services. The discussions unfolded at the parties’ respective offices and through telephone and email communications, culminating in a formal written agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} which is documented and referenced as Annexure A. The relationship between the parties thus began as a commercial negotiation that progressed into a contractual relationship upon execution of the Agreement (Annexure A).

2. On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the parties executed the Agreement/Contract (Annexure A). The terms as recorded in the Agreement provided that the Defendant would supply the contracted goods/services described in Annexure A and that the Plaintiff would make an advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000 toward performance of the contract. The Agreement set a performance date of {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} for the Defendant to commence and complete the required delivery, and it provided that, if performance was not completed, the Plaintiff would be entitled to the refund of the advance amount. This narrative reflects the understanding and obligations that the parties purported to undertake under the written contract (Annexure A).

3. In accordance with the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by bank transfer to the account designated by the Defendant. The payment was made in full and is evidenced by the bank-related documents in Annexure B. The transfer was intended as an advance toward performance of the contract and is described in Annexure B as part of the transaction between the Plaintiff and the Defendant.

4. The Defendant undertook to commence performance and deliver the contracted goods/services by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff undertook to assist as reasonably required, including providing any necessary confirmations or approvals to facilitate performance. These reciprocal obligations and the timeline for performance are reflected in the terms of the Agreement (Annexure A).

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform by the stipulated due date and failed to refund the Rs. 2,000,000 advance. Despite the Plaintiff’s repeated requests and communications seeking either delivery of the contracted goods/services or repayment of the advance, the Defendant did not deliver the goods/services nor return the advance amount. The non-performance and non-refund by the Defendant constitute the specific default described in the factual timeline of the contract and the related communications.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs within the specified period, and the notice is attached as Annexure C. The Defendant’s reply to the legal notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} and is contained in Annexure D. Following these communications, the Plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, seeking recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs, with supporting documentation referenced in Annexures A, B, C, D, and F as applicable. Annexure E contains any acknowledgment/admission document, if available, and Annexure F includes additional supporting documents.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a specific restitutionary obligation: when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, on {{DATE}} at {{PLACE}}, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} paid the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} Rs. 20,00,000 as an advance under the Agreement dated {{DATE}}, evidenced by Annexure A, with payment proof in Annexure B. The business transaction contemplated by that agreement failed due to the Defendant’s default in performance, resulting in total failure of consideration; the Defendant has retained the Rs. 20,00,000 without lawful justification and is thus obligated to restore the entire amount to the Plaintiff under the said provision. The legal consequence is restitution: the Defendant must refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000 together with interest and costs as may be fixed by the Court. The Plaintiff accordingly pleads for refund of Rs. 20,00,000 and for costs and interest, relying on Annexure A to show the contract, Annexure B to prove payment, and Annexure C (legal notice) and Annexure D (reply) to reflect the pre-suit dispute and Defendant’s default. The Defendant may advance defenses such as lack of a valid contract, or absence of any part performance or adjustment against the claim; in response, it is submitted that Annexure A confirms a binding contract, Annexure B confirms payment of the admitted advance, there is no forfeiture clause or set-off claim material to defeat restitution, and there is no counter-claim, so the defense cannot nullify the statutory restoration duty under Section 65. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court does not accept total failure of consideration as the basis for relief, the Plaintiff nonetheless seeks restoration of the amount under Section 65 as the proper remedy to prevent unjust enrichment and to place the Plaintiff in the position prior to payment, together with interest and costs. Annexure F may be relied upon for any additional supportive documents.

---SECTION_TEXT---


CAUSE OF ACTION

Origin of the cause of action arises on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Plaintiff's advance of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rs. 2,000,000) paid for the contemplated business transaction under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and to perform the obligations arising therefrom, thereby resulting in a total failure of consideration and giving the Plaintiff a right to seek repayment of the advance amount with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding refund of the advance, and the Defendant, upon receipt of the notice, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount, a position reflected in the Defendant's reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}; by this act the Defendant reinforced the continuing breach and the Plaintiff’s entitlement to relief. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 20,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and this retention persists as of the filing of the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization, with the Plaintiff thus seeking the immediate refund of the advance amount along with interest and costs to be awarded on the terms of the law. Place names and the manner of retention reflect the ongoing failure of performance by the Defendant and the Plaintiff’s continuing entitlement to the return of its funds. {{PLACE_NAME}} and {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} are referenced to establish the contractual nexus and the defendant’s obligations, while the notices and replies dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} illustrate the sequence of demands and non-compliance that sustain the action throughout the period leading up to filing. The Plaintiff relies on the facts set out here to establish a clear origin, a continued accrual, and the ongoing nature of the wrongful retention of the advance, with the remedy sought including refund, interest, and costs.


LIMITATION

The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, contending total failure of consideration due to the Defendant’s default. The Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to repay the advance in accordance with the contract, thereby giving rise to a cause of action in law. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, when the Defendant failed to perform its obligation. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963; the limitation period commences from the accrual date {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} (the date the cause of action first arose) and the suit has been filed within that period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

On {{FILING_DATE}} before {{COURT_NAME}} the Plaintiff values the suit at Rs. 2,000,000, being the principal amount claimed against the Defendant. This valuation is for purposes of jurisdiction and court fee computation. In accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, the court fee payable on the plaint is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated by applying the Act’s schedule to the suit value.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff seeks three distinct prayers in the interest claim. Pre-suit interest is claimed on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum for the period from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, arising from the Defendant’s failure to repay or perform in accordance with the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and the advance of Rs. 2,000,000 made on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} for the commercial transaction between the parties. The legal basis rests on contractual understanding and commercial practice that money paid or lent as part of a business transaction bears interest when the debtor fails to honor the obligation, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The period of pre-suit interest thus runs from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} up to the filing of suit on {{FILING_DATE}} and is justified by the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, as evidenced by the contemporaneous agreement and the parties’ conduct leading to demand for repayment.

Pendente lite interest is claimed at {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on Rs. 2,000,000 from the date of filing the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, currently anticipated on {{DECRE_DATE}}. This prayer is made under Section 34 CPC, which permits the Court to award interest on money claimed from the date of the suit until decree at a rate deemed just, not exceeding the contract or law. The rate is justified because the suit concerns a commercial transaction in which the Defendant has benefited from the use of the Plaintiff’s funds during the pendency of the litigation, and the Plaintiff has been deprived of the use of that money. The chosen rate reflects prevailing mercantile norms and, where applicable, the contractual understanding between the parties. The pendente lite period thus spans from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECRE_DATE}}, and the legal basis is Section 34 CPC, with the rate selected to reflect commercial reality and the risk of delay.

Future interest is sought on the decretal amount from the date of decree on {{DECRE_DATE}} until realization on {{REALIZATION_DATE}} at {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum. This post-decree interest is warranted to ensure full compensation for the continued withholding of the money after judgment and to secure the Plaintiff’s ability to recover the amount adjudged, consistent with the overarching objective of Section 34 CPC to compensate for the use of money until realisation. The rate chosen aligns with commercial practice and the need to encourage prompt satisfaction of the decree, and it is justified by the Defendant’s obligation to honor the decree in respect of the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 as adjudged by the Court.

In summary, the three prayers provide for (i) pre-suit interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum with the contractual/commercial justification; (ii) pendente lite interest from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECRE_DATE}} at {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC with justification grounded in the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention; and (iii) future post-decree interest from {{DECRE_DATE}} to {{REALIZATION_DATE}} at {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum to secure realization and reflect the Court’s mandate to compensate for the use of money until full realization.

---SECTION_TEXT---


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
(b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} up to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) Award pendente lite interest on the principal sum at the rate of {{PENDENTE_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree. 
(d) Order future interest at the rate of {{FUTURE_RATE}}% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} amounting to Rs. {{COSTS_AMOUNT}}/-. 
(f) Pass any other relief as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

7. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure A
8. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure B
9. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure C
10. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure D
11. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure E
12. Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}; Annexure Label: Annexure F


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No.: {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}
Date: {{FILING_DATE}}
